perturbation.original_text: EmployeeCo agrees to provide to the Operating Company and its affiliates the Services and such other services as may be determined by the Parties from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: EmployeeCo will use its best efforts to provide to the Operating Company and its affiliates the Services and such other services as may be determined by the Parties from time to time.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to provide' to 'will use its best efforts to provide' weakens EmployeeCo's obligation. Originally, EmployeeCo was strictly bound to provide the Services. With the modification, the obligation becomes less stringent. This creates an in-text contradiction because other sections of the contract still rely on EmployeeCo's original, strict obligation to provide services, especially concerning reimbursement and allocation based on those services.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Operating Company shall reimburse EmployeeCo for all direct or indirect costs and expenses incurred by EmployeeCo in connection with performing its obligations under this Agreement including, but not limited to: (a) salaries of the Services Employees; (b) the cost of employee benefits for the Services Employees, including 401(k), pension, bonuses and health insurance benefits (whether through insurance policies provided by third-parties or self-insurance); (c) costs associated with workers' compensation claims and other disputes or liabilities associated with the Services Employees; (d) severance costs with respect to any terminated Services Employees; and (e) all sales, use, employment, excise, value added or similar taxes, if any, that may be applicable from time to time with respect to the Services.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'agrees to provide' to 'will use its best efforts to provide' significantly weakens EmployeeCo's obligation to provide services. This directly contradicts the section regarding reimbursement, which is predicated on the assumption that EmployeeCo is strictly obligated to perform its obligations, as stated in the original agreement. If EmployeeCo only uses its 'best efforts' and fails to provide certain services, the basis for reimbursement becomes unclear and potentially unenforceable, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Such reimbursements shall be made by the Operating Company to EmployeeCo in advance or immediately upon such costs being incurred, or otherwise in accordance with historical practice, unless otherwise agreed between the Operating Company and EmployeeCo.
perturbation.changed_text: Such reimbursements will generally be made by the Operating Company to EmployeeCo at the Operating Company's discretion, considering historical practice and current financial conditions, unless otherwise agreed between the Operating Company and EmployeeCo.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces ambiguity by replacing the relatively clear reimbursement terms with a discretionary standard based on the Operating Company's judgment. The original text set forth clear expectations for reimbursement, which now introduces uncertainty in how the reimbursements are being done. This could potentially contract the original term which states EmployeeCo its obligated to be reimbused. This changes undermines the agreements obligations between the two companies
perturbation.contradicted_text: As an additional fee, the Operating Company shall also pay to EmployeeCo an amount equal to 1% of the amount of all reimbursements made under Section 1.2, payable at the same time as such reimbursements, unless otherwise agreed between the Operating Company and EmployeeCo.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated reimbursements 'in advance or immediately upon such costs being incurred,' creating a clear obligation. The changed text introduces discretion, stating reimbursements 'will generally be made...at the Operating Company's discretion.' This directly conflicts with the contradicted text, which calculates a fee based on 'all reimbursements made under Section 1.2, payable at the same time as such reimbursements.' The discretionary nature introduced in the changed text creates ambiguity and undermines the obligation to make reimbursements, thereby contradicting the clause that calculates fees based on 'all reimbursements'.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be terminated by (a) the written agreement of the Parties or (b) by either Party upon 5 days written notice to the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be terminated by (a) the written agreement of the Parties or (b) by either Party upon 5 days written notice to the other Party, provided that such termination does not disrupt ongoing services critical to the Operating Company's core business.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the clause 'provided that such termination does not disrupt ongoing services critical to the Operating Company's core business' introduces a condition that wasn't present before. This creates uncertainty as to when termination is truly possible. The original clause allowed for termination with 5 days' notice with no strings attached. The added clause now contradicts this, creating an in-text contradiction. Termination rights are now ambiguous, since 2.3 contradicts the original 5 days for termination by imposing a condition.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect until terminated by the Parties. This Agreement may be terminated by (a) the written agreement of the Parties or (b) by either Party upon 5 days written notice to the other Party. All payment obligations hereunder shall survive the termination of this Agreement in accordance with their respective terms.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows termination with 5 days' notice. The changed text adds a condition that termination cannot disrupt ongoing services. This creates an ambiguity and in-text contradiction as to when termination is actually possible, because now the 5-day notice may not be sufficient if it disrupts ongoing services. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction' where inconsistent definitions of key terms or obligations make enforcement difficult. The contradicted text specifies a simple '5 days written notice' is sufficient for termination, while the changed text makes the termination conditional, causing a direct conflict.
